issue: applyGroupElements rebases children by subtracting group.x/y (the min AABB corner), but for a rotated child the AABB corner is NOT the same as the child's top-left origin. The child's local position in the group becomes (element.x - minX, element.y - minY), which is correct for the unrotated case. However on ungroup the inverse adds group.x/y back. This round-trip is actually correct for x/y origin (top-left), but elementAabb is used to compute minX/minY, meaning the group origin is the min AABB corner, not the min origin corner. For a rotated element with origin at (50,50) but AABB.x = 20 (because the rotated corners extend left), the group.x = 20, and the child gets local x = 50 - 20 = 30, and on ungroup x = 30 + 20 = 50. This is correct. BUT the group extent (elementExtent for the group) is computed from elementAabb of children with the children already rebased — meaning the AABB computation inside elementExtent for groups is applied in group-local coords and does not account for the fact that a rotated child's top-left is not the same as its AABB corner. The group's own AABB (used for snap targets and letterbox) will be wrong for groups containing rotated elements.
action: In elementExtent for 'group', accumulate max of (child.x + extent.width, child.y + extent.height) — but this is currently calling elementAabb(child) in group-local space, which returns the AABB of the rotated child relative to the child's own origin. This is actually correct because elementAabb handles rotation. The issue: for group extent, we want max(aabb.x + aabb.width) in group-local space, which IS what the current code does. However, the origin of the AABB when the child is rotated can be negative (aabb.x < child.x when rotated). The current code only tracks maxX and maxY, not minX/minY. This means groups with children rotated such that their AABB extends to negative group-local coordinates will have an underestimated extent. Fix: track minX/minY of children's AABBs as well when computing group extent, and return {width: maxX - minX, height: maxY - minY} or ensure group origin from applyGroupElements captures the full min across all member AABB corners.
verification: Add a test where two rects at (0,0) each are rotated 45 degrees. After grouping, confirm elementExtent(group) returns the correct AABB that covers all rotated corners, not just (0..maxX, 0..maxY).

issue: deletePageCommand.undo uses add_page (restoreOp) with only {name, width, height, background} from the snapshot — it deliberately drops elements. This is documented as intentional. However the inverse operation returned by inverseOperations() is the same shell add_page — so any caller that emits inverseOperations() for server-side undo tracking will restore an empty page, silently losing all elements. The comment says 'server has the full document and can replay its own history' but that assumption may not hold for callers of inverseOperations() outside a server context.
action: Either (a) document on the SceneCommand contract that inverseOperations() for delete_page is not element-complete and callers must not use it for server-side replay, OR (b) capture and restore the full page including bleed and guides (not just the 4 opts fields). The snapshot already captures the full page — the options bag just needs to be extended or the inverse should use a different strategy (e.g. snapshot the page and re-add elements after the page is restored). Since this is already documented as a known WF2 open issue, the minimum fix is adding a JSDoc to inverseOperations() on the deletePageCommand return value noting the limitation.
verification: Caller code review: confirm no path emits command.inverseOperations() for delete_page and replays it against the server store expecting element restoration.

issue: In the stale-rev retry path, mintId is called on the second applySceneOperations call. If any operation in the batch is duplicate_page, mintId was already consumed for element ids during the first apply attempt. The retry calls mintId() again producing different ids than the first attempt. If the first attempt partially succeeded (e.g. the store wrote the row but the returning() call failed), the retry will produce different element ids, creating a divergence between what the server stored and what the caller's mintId counter is at.
action: The retry is safe ONLY because saveDocument is atomic — either it writes (and returns) or it doesn't. The scenario where a row is written but returning() fails is not possible with SQLite/D1 under normal operation. However, if mintId is a shared stateful counter, the counter is advanced by the first failed attempt. The caller's counter is now at N+M (where M = number of duplicate_page elements minted). On retry, it starts at N+M and mints new ids. This is correct behavior — the retry produces a fresh document with fresh ids. But the caller received element_id: id-N from the tool response only AFTER success. Since the tool returns element_id built from mintId() before applyPlan, if the first attempt fails the returned element_id was never persisted. The tool returns element_id AFTER a successful applyPlan, so this is fine. Document the invariant: mintId is allowed to advance monotonically even across retry; the tool response element_id is only returned on success.
verification: Review mcp-tools.ts add_text/add_image/add_shape: confirm element id is minted before applyPlan, and the tool only returns element_id when applyPlan resolves (no partial success path).

issue: elementExtent for 'group' computes maxX = max(aabb.x + aabb.width) and maxY = max(aabb.y + aabb.height) over child AABBs, starting with maxX=0, maxY=0. If ALL children have AABB origins at negative x or y (possible for rotated elements placed near the group origin), maxX and maxY will start at 0 and the result will be the correct range. However, if a child's AABB.x is negative (e.g., a 45-degree rotated rect whose top-left is at group-local (0,0) extends to AABB.x = -35), aabb.x + aabb.width could still be positive. But the starting 0 ignores the fact that the group extent should also cover the negative-x side. The extent should be maxX - minX (where minX tracks the minimum AABB.x). Currently minX is implicitly 0. Fix is consistent with finding #1.
action: Track minX=0 and minY=0 as the implicit group origin, but also track min of aabb.x across children (not just max of aabb.x+width). Return width = max(maxX, 0) - min(minX, 0) to handle children whose AABBs extend leftward or upward of the group origin.
verification: Unit test: group containing a rect at (0,0) rotated 45 degrees. elementExtent(group) should return width/height covering all four rotated corners, not just the positive quadrant.

issue: In add_text, env.mintId is called twice: once to mint the element id (line 295: const id = env.mintId()), and again as the mintId argument to applyPlan (line 317: applyPlan(env.store, env.mintId, ...)). applyPlan passes mintId to storeApplyScenePlan which passes it to applySceneOperations as the id factory for duplicate_page. For add_text (an add_element op, not duplicate_page), mintId is never called inside applySceneOperations. So the double reference is harmless for add_text but creates a latent confusion: any reader might think mintId is called again inside applyPlan for add_text. Same pattern in add_image, add_shape, add_video, add_page, duplicate_page. For duplicate_page specifically, mintId IS consumed inside applySceneOperations to re-mint element ids — and the outer id = env.mintId() for the page id is correct. This is not a bug but a readability hazard.
action: No code change needed. Add a comment above applyPlan calls in add_text/add_image/add_shape/add_video noting that mintId is passed for the retry path and for duplicate_page element re-minting, but is not called for non-duplicate operations.
verification: Confirm env.mintId is not called during applySceneOperations for any op except duplicate_page.

issue: reorderElementCommand captures fromIndex in execute() via a mutable closure variable (capturedFromIndex). If execute() is called multiple times (which SceneCommandStack.redo() does — it calls command.execute again), the captured index is overwritten with the index from the second call's document state, which is the already-reordered state. The second execute call's from-index will be wrong — it will capture toIndex (since after redo the element is at toIndex). The undo after a second execute will then reorder to the wrong position.
action: The standard pattern for redo-safe commands is to capture state only on the FIRST execute or to recompute the inverse each time. Fix: capture capturedFromIndex once and guard with 'if (capturedFromIndex === null)' so subsequent execute calls (via redo) do not overwrite it. But this means after undo→redo→undo the second undo uses the original from-index, which is correct. However redo calls execute again which re-captures the from-index from the new current document, breaking the invariant. The real fix is to make the command stateless by accepting the from-index at construction time (the caller knows the current state) or by not using redo via the same execute path. Same issue exists in reorderPageCommand.
verification: Test: create a 3-element list, execute reorderElementCommand(r1 toIndex=2), undo (restores), redo (calls execute again), undo again — verify r1 is back at index 0 not some other index.

issue: validateReorderElement checks toIndex against owner.length with 'toIndex >= owner.length' — but after the element is removed from the array, the valid target range is 0..length-1 (length-1 positions). Before removal, the owner has N elements; after removal it has N-1, so valid indices are 0..N-2 = 0..owner.length-2. The check 'toIndex >= owner.length' allows toIndex = owner.length - 1 = N-1, which after removal becomes index N-1 in an N-1 element array — a valid append-to-end position. In applyReorderElement, splice(index, 1) removes the element (length becomes N-1), then splice(toIndex, 0, element) inserts at toIndex. If toIndex = N-1 (owner.length - 1), this inserts at position N-1 in an N-1 element array, which is valid (appends to end). So the check is actually correct for the intended semantics of 'move to this index in the result'. No bug.
action: No change needed. Add a comment in validateReorderElement explaining that toIndex is the target index in the post-remove array, so owner.length-1 is the valid max (the element will be the last item).
verification: Existing test 'reorder_element: toIndex out of range' covers the rejection. Add a test for toIndex = owner.length - 1 (valid, moves element to last position).

issue: requirePageElement is defined but never called — the function at line 47 is dead code. All command factories call requirePage + requireElement directly.
verification: TypeScript will not error on removal; grep for requirePageElement to confirm no callers.

issue: optionalPositiveInteger is named 'positive' but accepts 0 (value >= 0). The validation is 'value < 0' (rejects negatives), making it non-negative, not positive. The name is misleading.
action: Rename to optionalNonNegativeInteger, or change the check to 'value < 0' -> 'value <= 0' if zero should be excluded. For 'index' parameters (0-based), 0 is a valid value, so non-negative is correct semantics. Rename only.
verification: Rename and grep all call sites for optionalPositiveInteger to update.

issue: isCrossOriginSrc returns true for all http(s) URLs including same-origin ones (e.g., https://app.tangle.tools/api/image.png). It is documented as a heuristic that only runs after a SecurityError fires. The comment is correct. No functional bug — just noted that the heuristic produces false positives for same-origin https assets when the SecurityError is triggered by a different image.
action: No change needed. Document clearly that identifyTaintedSrc returns the first cross-origin-looking src as a best-effort attribution, not a definitive diagnosis.
verification: Existing tests cover the documented semantics.
